headlines.title: San Francisco transit overwhelmed: July 4 crowds cause hours-long BART, Caltrain and Waymo delays
headlines.description: More than 100,000 people crowded San Francisco’s waterfront Saturday for the Fourth of July fireworks show, but getting home proved to be a grueling ordeal as transit systems and city streets were pushed far beyond capacity. Riders reported that typical 30-minute trips stretched into waits of two to four hours. BART lines wrapped around stations, Caltrain platforms were packed and stalled Waymo autonomous vehicles caused further gridlock in downtown streets. City leaders and transit agencies admitted that major adjustments are needed before San Francisco hosts another large-scale event. “Our transportation system simply could not handle the volume of people that arrived in San Francisco on Saturday,” said Viktoriya Wise of the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency. The SFMTA reported 41,000 more boardings than last Fourth of July — a 13% increase that caused some trips to take two to three times longer than normal. “We did everything we could to move as many people as possible and made adjustments in the field to our transit operations to pull additional service and get people home as quickly as we possibly could,” Wise said. BART rider Gloria Zhu described the chaotic scene at the stations. “There’s only so much you can do before you just kind of accept your fate,” Zhu said. “It was just push and shove into the trains, so we moved with the people and hoped to get on a train before the last one was out.” Caltrain also faced an unexpected surge, carrying more than 14,000 riders and prompting officials to scramble for extra capacity. “We planned on running an additional train Saturday night,” said Caltrain spokesman Dan Lieberman. “Turns out we needed more than that.” The congestion was compounded by dozens of Waymo vehicles that stopped throughout downtown, creating bottlenecks that worsened the already difficult commute. Wise noted that the city plans to address the disruption caused by the robotaxis. “We will be closely working with Waymo to better understand how we can have special events without interruptions from them,” Wise said. The mayor’s office stated that public safety remained the top priority and noted that officials had encouraged attendees to take public transit instead of driving. However, as the mass exodus began, riders argued the system was ill-equipped to keep up. Across the board, city agencies say they will review staffing, traffic control and transit operations in hopes of preventing a repeat during the next major event.

headlines.title: 5 arrested in $100K smash-and-grab jewelry robbery at Concord's Sun Valley Mall
headlines.description: Police arrested five suspected gang members following a brazen smash-and-grab robbery at an East Bay mall that netted more than $100,000 in jewelry, authorities said. The takedown resulted from a coordinated effort between the San Francisco and Concord police departments, utilizing drones and new real-time crime centers to track the suspects across the Bay Area. On June 28, the San Francisco Police Department’s Violence Reduction Team was monitoring the group when the suspects traveled to Concord and targeted a jewelry kiosk inside the Sun Valley Mall. Concord Police Detective Dylan Luciano said the suspects used sledgehammers to smash the display cases during the heist. “One of the suspects was armed with pepper spray and actually deployed that pepper spray towards the employees, and they stole well over $100,000 worth of jewelry,” Luciano said. Following the robbery, investigators used drones to track the suspects from Concord to Richmond. Officers observed the men dumping bags filled with the stolen jewelry and other evidence before moving in to make the arrests. San Francisco police released dramatic video of the felony traffic stop, which showed officers recovering at least one handgun at the scene. Subsequent searches of homes tied to the suspects uncovered several assault rifles, police said. The Sun Valley Mall has been a frequent target for retail theft, and the recent arrests brought a wave of relief to shoppers. “I think it’s a good thing that the police are taking action to deal with the robbery that took place here,” shopper Zenzi Asiedu said. Shopper Marion Barnor echoed that sentiment, adding, “I’m very glad and proud of the officers and police for doing their job exceptionally well.” Law enforcement officials credited the successful arrests to new regional tactics and cross-agency collaboration. “It’s obviously important for our community, for our city, and for our economy for people to feel welcome to safely shop at the Sun Valley Mall,” Luciano said. “It obviously feels good to get criminals that come into our community off the street.”

headlines.title: Man charged with attempted murder in Oakland vehicle attack
headlines.description: A man has been charged with attempted murder after prosecutors say he “intentionally” drove his vehicle into a crowd of pedestrians following an Oakland First Friday event in May, severely injuring several people. Alameda County District Attorney Ursula Jones Dickson announced the criminal complaint Monday against Dameon Denzel Clay. “This case involves an intentional act of extreme violence in which the defendant drove his car onto a sidewalk, striking multiple victims and causing serious injuries,” Dickson said in a statement. The crash occurred at about 1:37 a.m. on May 2 in the 500 block of 18th Street. According to the investigation, Oakland police received a barrage of emergency calls reporting that a vehicle had plowed into a group of people. Prosecutors allege Clay deliberately drove onto the sidewalk and accelerated toward the crowd, striking approximately eight people before crashing into a gate. Authorities say Clay then abandoned the vehicle and fled the scene on foot. Several victims sustained serious injuries in the attack. Clay is scheduled to be arraigned Tuesday in Department 112 of the Alameda County Superior Court.

headlines.title: East Bay fire departments assessing Fourth of July fireworks-related damage
headlines.description: The Fourth of July may be over, but the damage from illegal fireworks is still being counted. In Oakland, firefighters responded to multiple fireworks-related incidents, including five structure fires. One home was left with significant damage. Video also shows cars doing donuts while passengers fired roman candles into the street. At 96th Avenue and Plymouth Street, witnesses said illegal fireworks sparked a tree fire that spread to two vehicles and damaged power lines. Authorities said firefighters spent more than an hour putting out the flames. Despite seeing a lot of activity, the Oakland Fire Department said this year the celebrations caused less damage than previous Fourth of July celebrations. Police said enforcement actions took place in areas known for illegal firework activity. Over the weekend, Oakland police made 20 arrests, recovered eight firearms and issued 184 citations unrelated to fireworks. However, police said many of the enforcement actions took place in areas where illegal fireworks were being used and said additional fireworks-related citations could still be issued. “Ongoing investigations will continue to ramp up and look at those individuals that partook in dangerous behavior, Oakland Police Department Capt. Dave Burke said. “We will continue those investigations and try to bring those individuals to justice as we move forward.” San Francisco Jul 5 San Jose Jul 4 San Francisco Jun 30 The Alameda County Fire Department responded to about 33 fireworks-related incidents on the Fourth of July, including four fires caused by improperly disposed fireworks — one of which damaged a home. Meanwhile, Oakland officials are reminding residents all fireworks are illegal and urging people to report dangerous activity before it leads to another fire. “They can have a profound affect on the elderly, youth and also animals,” Burke said. “Community members continue to call and give us those locations where they are being inflicted by that dangerous behavior. We will respond accordingly.”

headlines.title: San Jose pastor convicted of domestic violence for 2024 attack
headlines.description: A jury last week convicted a San Jose pastor of domestic violence, the Santa Clara County District Attorney’s Office announced Monday. Ray Fitzgerald Montgomery is the former executive director of People Acting in Community Together (PACT). After three hours of jury deliberations the 59-year-old was found guilty of misdemeanor domestic violence against a 42-year-old woman, with whom he had a relationship with, in his home in 2024. According to the DA’s office, Montgomery “picked the victim up and dragged her down the hallway. She told him to stop, but he refused, eventually beginning to strangle her by pressing her neck between his forearm and bicep. Around that time, she felt she was about to lose consciousness when she felt him release her, causing her to then collapse on the floor.” The woman was able to run and hide in a bathroom to call for help, authorities said. The next day, she went to Stanford Hospital and reported the domestic violence. Before Montgomery was charged, he filed a lawsuit against the woman, claiming she was defaming him by falsely accusing him of the attack. On Nov. 26, 2024, the victim identified her alleged abuser to San Jose police and later provided additional statements to investigators, authorities said. Montgomery is set to be sentenced on September 8, 2026, in Department 45 of the Hall of Justice in San Jose.

headlines.title: As youth soccer grows in the Bay area, some kids still struggle for opportunities to play
headlines.description: Neha Ma was 7 years old when she arrived in the United States from a refugee camp in northern Thailand. She only knew three English words at the time, she said. Soccer became her lifeline in America, where everything from the language to the culture was new, she said. The sport connected her with other Asian communities thousands of miles away from home, and the team eventually taught her English. “Soccer wasn’t just all about soccer,” Ma said. “It was a whole new community, mentorship, family.” Ma’s childhood club, Soccer Without Borders, is an international nonprofit that offers free soccer programs and educational opportunities, such as language learning, for immigrant youth across the world — including in the Bay Area. While programs like Soccer Without Borders are working to make the sport inclusive, soccer still has an accessibility problem. Although more than 800 new soccer fields have been created in the area since 2014, they aren’t distributed evenly among neighborhoods. Some children must still scramble to find a field. var pymParent = new pym.Parent('map-over-time', 'https://visuals.otsops.com/projects/soccer-fields/map-over-time.html', {title: '', parenturlparam: 'parentDomain', parenturlvalue: window.location.hostname}); “Roughly 28 million children in the U.S. don’t have a place to play within walking distance of them,” said Lex Chalat, the executive director at U.S. Soccer’s Soccer Forward Foundation. Why most budding soccer players quit by age 14 Soccer in the United States, unlike in many other countries, operates largely on a pay-to-play model. According to the 2025 State of Play report from the Aspen Institute, the average U.S. family spends $1,016 on their child’s primary sport, up nearly 50% in five years. Ma’s brother, Swahid Si, said that soccer is everything to him. So he appreciates that Soccer Without Borders’s programs are free. “They give out cleat donations, they give out shorts, shoes, all that,” he said. “If somebody wants to join and play, they can play.” Outdoor soccer participation for all ages in the United States was at an all-time high in 2025 at nearly 16 million, according to the Sports & Fitness Industry Association. Kids have been participating in fewer sports over the past few years, but children ages 6 to 12 remain the country’s largest group of outdoor soccer players with 5.5 million participating. By the time they reach age 14, however, 70% of them quit, especially those from underserved communities, U.S. Soccer found. “If I had to guess it would be because at 14 it probably gets really, really expensive,” said Tyler Adams, the captain of the U.S. soccer team at the 2022 FIFA World Cup and a member of this year’s team. “You’re spending a lot of time in the car, you’re spending a lot of money on flights, going to tournaments. Other sports are expensive as well, but soccer is especially expensive at a certain level.” Ma said that Soccer Without Borders’ siblings, parents, and coaches lessen transportation costs by giving each other rides. Everyone helps each other out, she said. Low-income neighborhoods face barriers to access There are now six times more fields in the Bay Area for each elementary-aged child than in 2014. But whether that means easier access to the soccer fields is determined by where a child lives. var pymParent = new pym.Parent('map-choropleth', 'https://visuals.otsops.com/projects/soccer-fields/map-choropleth.html', {title: '', parenturlparam: 'parentDomain', parenturlvalue: window.location.hostname}); We compared school districts in and around the Bay Area on key demographics and found that, perhaps unsurprisingly, soccer fields are concentrated in higher-income areas. Elementary school-aged children in areas with a low median household income have access to half the number of soccer fields as those in areas with a high median household income. In areas with the highest median income, an average of 740 kids share one soccer field. In areas with the lowest median income, that number goes up to 1,518. var pymParent = new pym.Parent('slope-chart-wrapper', 'https://visuals.otsops.com/projects/soccer-fields/slope-chart.html', {title: '', parenturlparam: 'parentDomain', parenturlvalue: window.location.hostname}); Access also varies by majority ethnicity. Children in the least white school districts have access to a third fewer soccer fields than children in the most white school districts. Latino and Black children are three times more likely than white children to stop playing soccer because they feel unwelcome, according to research by McKinsey and the U.S. Soccer Federation. The Trust for Public Land found that in the 100 largest U.S. cities on average, neighborhoods of color had access to 44% less park acreage than predominantly white neighborhoods. Si said the players on his team come from diverse backgrounds, ranging from Latino to Asian to Ethiopian. “We all bond over soccer, and that’s about it. Because you’re just a person, just a human, all just kicking around a ball,” he said. The ‘ideal’ soccer field In the Antioch Unified School District, as many as 14,480 kids share three soccer fields. var pymParent = new pym.Parent('neighborhoods', 'https://visuals.otsops.com/projects/soccer-fields/all_markets_html/neighborhoods/neighborhoods.html', {title: '', parenturlparam: 'parentDomain', parenturlvalue: window.location.hostname}); Source: OpenStreetMap, Census data “I wouldn’t say that anywhere in America really has direct access to soccer pitches unless you live in a major city,” Adams said. “For me, it was always walking down to a park and making the baseball field and the outfield the ‘ideal’ soccer field, or we’d play on the concrete of a basketball court. That’s just kind of how it was where I grew up.” Adams spent his childhood in New York’s Hudson Valley. The existence of a field doesn’t mean that it’s usable. It may not be in a setting that makes some would-be players feel that they belong, Chalat said, or the space may not be maintained. Homeless individuals often camp out at the field where her brother practices, and players have reported being verbally harassed or having their personal belongings stolen, Ma said. Much of the now-soccer fields in the area used to be open land or fields built for other sports. The soccer fields at The Crossing at East Cut in downtown San Francisco, for example, were built to replace a temporary Transbay Terminal in 2021. var pymParent = new pym.Parent('fields_post_2014', 'https://visuals.otsops.com/projects/soccer-fields/all_markets_html/fields_post_2014/fields_post_2014.html', {title: '', parenturlparam: 'parentDomain', parenturlvalue: window.location.hostname}); Source: OpenStreetMap, Census data Future of soccer: ‘Sustainable, community-centered, and accessible’ Ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, the U.S. Soccer Foundation has built mini-pitches in the Bay Area to provide more spaces for children to play in their neighborhoods. Chalat said Soccer Forward’s next phase is to make soccer truly accessible to everyone everywhere. “It’s not only public policy, it’s not only lack of investment, it’s not only a representation issue or a socioeconomic issue, it’s everything,” she said. “The key piece there is making sure it’s sustainable, that it’s community centered, and that it’s accessible. And that’s not an easy thing to do.” Other groups focused on making soccer more accessible include the nonprofit Street Soccer USA, which aims to provide an alternative to the pay-to-play model, especially in neighborhoods where over a third of residents live below the poverty line. Another organization, 703 Warriors, a youth soccer development club based in Arlington, Virginia, identified five key ways to meet marginalized communities, including connecting with them in more languages. “In those inner-city communities, they go through a lot of obstacles that not every kid in the suburbs or other kids have to go through,” Adams said. “I think there’s a lot of opportunity to do more wrong sometimes than there is good. It’s about inspiring them and remembering to find their reason why.” Methodology We analyzed OpenStreetMap data from 2014 and 2026 to get the number of fields across the U.S. and mapped it against American Community Survey 5-year estimates for unified school districts from 2014 and 2024 (the latest data available). We calculated the change in fields over time and across geographical boundaries. We mean ‘elementary school-aged kids’ to be the population group that is 5-14 years of age. ‘White’ represents the population that is white, non-Hispanic—all other races are grouped as non-white. For income calculations, we used median household income for families with children under 18 years of age. We split it into deciles to ensure we had the same number of geographical blocks in each category, with ‘lowest income’ being the first decile and ‘highest income’ being the last. ‘Low income’ and ‘high income’ are the groups below and above median household income for all school districts respectively. If an area did not have an estimation of median household income, we dropped that area. We did the same for the percentage of white population in a given geographical area, with ‘least white’ being the first decile and ‘most white’ being the last. Lastly, for our graphics, we limited our analysis to the school districts served by NBC and Telemundo Bay Area.

headlines.title: Seasonably cool week continues for the Bay Area, but beach hazards increase
headlines.description: Seasonably cool weather will continue across the Bay Area before a warming trend arrives during the second half of the week, while beachgoers are being warned of dangerous surf conditions beginning Tuesday. A persistent marine layer will continue bringing low clouds inland each evening and overnight before retreating toward the coast during the morning and afternoon, according to a National Weather Service advisory on Monday. Temperatures are expected to remain near or below seasonal averages through Tuesday before gradually climbing later in the week. The greatest concern is along California’s Pacific coast, where a long-period southwest swell is expected to increase the risk of sneaker waves, strong rip currents and breaking waves up to 10 feet from Tuesday through Friday. Forecasters warned south and southwest-facing beaches in the North Bay, San Francisco, the Peninsula, and Santa Cruz and Monterey counties face the highest risk. The weather service urged inexperienced swimmers and boaters to stay out of the water and warned that long-period swells can produce powerful waves over jetties, rocks and other areas that may appear safe. Beachgoers are also reminded never to turn their backs on the ocean.

headlines.title: World Cup watch parties wind down in San Jose's San Pedro Square in Team USA loss to Belgium
headlines.description: San Jose’s San Pedro Square was once again the center of attention Monday as crowds gathered to watch the United States take on Belgium in a World Cup knockout match. Kickoff for the Round of 16 clash in Seattle was set for 5 p.m. Belgium triumphed over the USA 4-1 and will now meet Spain in the quarterfinals as the USMNT played their worst game of the tournament. Following a stunning reversal by FIFA, U.S. striker Folarin Balogun was allowed to play after his one-game red-card ban was suspended. San Pedro Square was quieter than normal on Sunday after San Jose moved its official watch parties for the Mexico-England match to Discovery Meadow and SAP Center due to safety concerns and the need for more space. Belgium will face Spain on Friday. 2026 World Cup Jul 5 2026 World Cup Jul 5 2026 World Cup 18 hours ago FIFA dismisses Belgium's appeal and will let Folarin Balogun play for U.S.
headlines.source: Raj Mathai, Candice Nguyen and Miryam Villarreal

headlines.title: Unlawful assembly declared in downtown San Jose due to crowd's ‘unruly behavior'
headlines.description: San Jose police declared an unlawful assembly in downtown Sunday night in response to a crowd of people engaging in “unruly behavior,” the department said. Police said just after 10 p.m. roughly 200 to 300 people took over the area of East San Fernando and South Second streets for well over an hour, resulting in traffic disruptions. The crowd was setting off fireworks, drinking in the streets and “engaging in other unruly behavior,” police said. When officers tried to disperse the crowd, several people started to throw bottles and other objects at them, prompting the incident commander to issue an unlawful assembly dispersal order, police said. The crowd listened to the order and cleared the area, police said. It was not immediately known if any arrests were made. San Jose 16 hours ago San Jose Jul 5 1 dead, 1 detained after early morning San Jose shooting on Bonita Avenue

headlines.title: Man injured in downtown San Jose shooting
headlines.description: A man was injured in a shooting in downtown San Jose early Monday morning, according to police. The shooting happened at about 1:48 a.m. in the area of South First and San Salvador streets, police said. The victim, who suffered non-life-threatening injuries in the shooting, was found at a local hospital, according to police. Officers detained a man who they believe was connected to the shooting and recovered a firearm, police said. An investigation is ongoing.

headlines.title: Firefighters battle 10-acre fire southwest of San Jose
headlines.description: Firefighters were battling a 10-acre fire southwest of San Jose Sunday afternoon, according to Cal Fire. Cal Fire said aviation and ground resources were on the scene of the McKean Fire, which has burned 10 acres in the area of Almaden Road and Mountain Drive, southwest of San Jose. Officials said the flames were threatening four homes. Authorities said the fire has a moderate rate of spread and asked the public to avoid the rea to allow fire equipment and personnel safe access. Authorities said the forward progress have been stopped.

headlines.title: San Francisco's fireworks show ends in hours of gridlock involving Waymo cars
headlines.description: San Francisco’s Fourth of July celebration along the city’s waterfront quickly turned into hours of gridlock, dangerous fireworks incidents and a surge in emergency calls. Thousands packed Crissy Field to watch fireworks launched from the Golden Gate Bridge. But once the show ended, it wasn’t exactly a smooth ride home. While many left with great memories, others found themselves stuck for hours in gridlock, caught in a massive backup involving dozens of Waymo cars, while firefighters across the city were responding to fires and serious fireworks injuries. For Rose Peterson, the ride home after San Francisco’s Fourth of July fireworks took an unexpected turn. “We were pulling up to a four-way stop and this guy was shooting off a firework in the middle of the road, and then our Waymo starts driving, and we’re like ‘Wait, what’s happening?’” she said. No one inside was hurt, but Peterson said the experience left her questioning how autonomous vehicles respond in unpredictable situations. “I definitely think it needs to be more sensitive of anything that can come into the path of the road,” she said. That wasn’t the only challenge after the show. As thousands left Crissy Field and the Presidio, traffic came to a standstill. Among those stuck was Dave Guingona, who had friends visiting from the Philippines and wanted to show them a memorable Fourth of July in San Francisco. “Five minutes, 10 minutes, 15 minutes went by, then we realized people were getting out of their cars, yelling and screaming at these Waymos because there were no drivers,” he said. He said nearly two hours later, Waymo employees finally began driving the vehicles out. “They had a great time, but this is what they’re going to remember for the rest of their lives. This was unbelievable,” he said. While traffic barely moved, San Francisco firefighters responded to more than 500 calls for service, including four fireworks-related wildfires, numerous outside fires across the city and a vehicle fire on Connecticut Street believed to have been sparked by illegal fireworks. Crews also responded to two life-altering fireworks injuries. Capt. Jonathan Baxter said the incident Peterson experienced in the Waymo was another reminder of how quickly things could have turned tragic. “That situation could’ve had a far worse outcome—not only for the individuals in the car that could’ve been injured or worse—but for the individuals who lit the fireworks. They could have their life altered for the rest of their lives as well,” Baxter said. Waymo said extreme traffic congestion disrupted operations for several vehicles after the fireworks and crews worked with city officials to clear them from the area. The company also said it’s evaluating the incident surrounding fireworks, adding it’s committed to learning from situations like this. San Francisco fire officials are reminding people that even fireworks marketed as “safe” can cause devastating injuries and fires.
